verses.text: Jonathan's soul is 'knit with the soul of David,' and he loves him as himself, sealing it with a covenant and giving David his own robe and sword. The king's son gladly steps aside for the shepherd God has chosen. Selfless love rejoices in another's rise.

David succeeds everywhere, and the women sing, 'Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands.' From that day Saul 'eyed David.' Jealousy is born when another's praise feels like our loss. Saul cannot bear to be second, even to God's anointed.

David succeeds everywhere, and the women sing, 'Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands.' From that day Saul 'eyed David.' Jealousy is born when another's praise feels like our loss. Saul cannot bear to be second, even to God's anointed.

A tormenting spirit comes on Saul, and twice he hurls his javelin to pin David to the wall, but David escapes. Envy unchecked turns murderous. The harp that once soothed the king now plays before a man bent on killing the very one who comforts him.

A tormenting spirit comes on Saul, and twice he hurls his javelin to pin David to the wall, but David escapes. Envy unchecked turns murderous. The harp that once soothed the king now plays before a man bent on killing the very one who comforts him.

Saul fears David, 'because the LORD was with him,' and sends him off to lead the troops — yet David only prospers, and 'all Israel and Judah loved David.' What we try to push down, God may be lifting up. Saul's schemes to diminish David only enlarge him.

Saul offers his daughter Merab, secretly hoping the Philistines will kill David in the wars her hand requires. He hides murder behind a wedding. But God guards His servant, and in the end Merab is given to another. Man's plots cannot reach the one God shields.

Saul learns his daughter Michal loves David and sees in it 'a snare' — another way to send David to his death. A father who should bless his child's love instead twists it into a weapon. Sin will exploit even tender things for its dark ends.

Saul learns his daughter Michal loves David and sees in it 'a snare' — another way to send David to his death. A father who should bless his child's love instead twists it into a weapon. Sin will exploit even tender things for its dark ends.

Saul's servants flatter David into seeking the marriage, and the dowry demanded is a hundred Philistine foreskins — Saul hoping David will die collecting them. Hatred dresses itself up as honour. But David, poor and humble, takes the dangerous task in stride, trusting God.

David goes out and brings back not one hundred but two hundred, and Saul must give him Michal to wife. The plot meant to destroy him becomes the means of his advancement. God keeps turning the king's traps into David's stepping-stones.

David goes out and brings back not one hundred but two hundred, and Saul must give him Michal to wife. The plot meant to destroy him becomes the means of his advancement. God keeps turning the king's traps into David's stepping-stones.

Saul sees that the LORD is with David and that Michal loves him, and grows still more afraid, becoming David's enemy continually. Yet David 'behaved himself wisely,' and his name was precious. Walking wisely under hatred, he leaves his vindication to God.
